tweets.title: There are two types of airforces : Rich and Poor News : It’s official: F-35s are now being delivered without radars due to delays in the AN/APG-85 AESA radar program. It highlights two broad approaches to defence procurement. ✅ One approach is followed by countries that buy finished products. They expect complete, ready-to-fight systems because their military’s job is to fight wars, not participate in weapon development. ✅ The other approach is followed by countries that treat suppliers as long-term partners. They understand that complex defence programs will face engineering and supply-chain disruptions, especially during periods of heightened geopolitical tension. These airforces are “poor” and do not see importing an alternative at will is often an option, they work through the delays together rather than expecting perfection from day one.
tweets.content:
There are two types of airforces : Rich and Poor

News : It’s official: F-35s are now being delivered without radars due to delays in the AN/APG-85 AESA radar program.

It highlights two broad approaches to defence procurement.

✅ One approach is followed by countries that buy finished products. They expect complete, ready-to-fight systems because their military’s job is to fight wars, not participate in weapon development.

✅ The other approach is followed by countries that treat suppliers as long-term partners. They understand that complex defence programs will face engineering and supply-chain disruptions, especially during periods of heightened geopolitical tension.

These airforces are “poor” and do not see importing an alternative at will is often an option, they work through the delays together rather than expecting perfection from day one.

The plant uses the indigenously developed Copper-Chlorine (Cu-Cl) Thermochemical Cycle, driven by heat from the Fast Breeder Test Reactor (FBTR), to produce hydrogen without relying solely on electricity. tweets.content:
BREAKING: India Unveils World's First Nuclear Heat-Based Hydrogen Production Facility

India has commissioned the world's first hydrogen production facility powered by nuclear process heat, at IGCAR, Kalpakkam.

The plant uses the indigenously developed Copper-Chlorine (Cu-Cl) Thermochemical Cycle, driven by heat from the Fast Breeder Test Reactor (FBTR), to produce hydrogen without relying solely on electricity.

Developed jointly by BARC and IGCAR, the facility serves as a technology demonstrator and marks a major step toward clean hydrogen production, energy security, and India's decarbonisation goals.

Netram Defence Review (@NetramDefence)

Kishtwar Incident: Army's Version of Events (Summary)

According to the Army's account, the incident began after Rashtriya Rifles (RR) civilian vehicles, engaged in official duties, inadvertently delayed the Deputy Commissioner's convoy by 10–20 minutes. Following the delay, the civil administration allegedly ordered the immediate seizure of the Army vehicles, which were taken to Atholi Police Station.

The Army claims the seizure was carried out without following the prescribed Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs). In an effort to resolve the matter peacefully, the Commanding Officer reportedly sent a Company Commander and a Quick Reaction Team (QRT) to the police station to seek the release of the vehicles through dialogue.

The Army alleges that police personnel locked the station gates and declined repeated requests for discussion. It further claims that after attempts at communication failed, the gates were breached. During the confrontation, a police official allegedly cocked his weapon and threatened the Army personnel, prompting the QRT to intervene, disarm the individual, and secure the area. A physical altercation followed, involving police personnel, including the Station House Officer (SHO).

The Army maintains that the subsequent FIR filed against its personnel resulted from a breakdown in coordination between the civil administration and the Army. The matter remains under investigation, and the claims made by both sides have not been judicially established.

A major security scare has emerged from Chirawa, Rajasthan, after a man allegedly posing as an Indian Army Lieutenant Colonel and ECHS Vigilance Officer gained access to multiple private hospitals and examined sensitive records related to ex-servicemen and their families. The suspect reportedly convinced hospital administrations with his knowledge of military healthcare procedures and inspected OPD, admission, and treatment records before raising suspicion.

The alarm was triggered when hospital authorities noticed inconsistencies in his conduct and questioned his credentials. After ECHS officials intervened and attempted verification, the suspect failed to answer technical military questions and fled the area before he could be detained. A report has now been forwarded to Army Headquarters, while security agencies investigate whether this was an attempted fraud, data collection operation, or part of a larger network exploiting military identity.

The incident has exposed serious concerns regarding the misuse of military uniforms, unauthorized access to sensitive records, and verification protocols at institutions dealing with defence personnel and veterans.

tweets.content:
DRDO’s Archer-NG UAV to Gain Strike Capability

India's indigenous Archer-NG UAV is being developed beyond its surveillance role into a multi-role combat platform capable of engaging tanks, armoured vehicles, and even aerial targets.

Key reported upgrades include:

1. Integration of anti-tank guided missiles (ATGMs) and Smart Anti-Airfield Weapons (SAAW)

2. Planned integration of the Astra Mk-1 beyond-visual-range air-to-air missile (BVRAAM) for engaging hostile aircraft

3. AESA radar to enhance target detection, tracking, and engagement

4. Retention of its primary intelligence, surveillance, target acquisition, and reconnaissance (ISTAR) role while adding precision-strike capability.

If these capabilities are successfully integrated and validated, the Archer-NG will become one of India's first indigenous UAVs capable of performing both surveillance and offensive combat missions, significantly expanding the operational flexibility of the Indian Armed Forces.
